content:
{
  "title": "Graph-of-Thought Prompting: Structuring LLM Mathematical Reasoning via Dynamic Dependency Graphs",
  "problem": "Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and its variants guide LLMs through stepwise mathematical reasoning, but often fail when multi-step dependencies or interrelated subproblems exist. LLMs lack mechanisms to explicitly track and update variable dependencies during complex problem solving.",
  "motivation": "Performance on advanced mathematical questions requires not only sequential reasoning but also the coordination of interdependent sub-results—a frequent source of LLM errors. Enabling LLMs to dynamically manage and refer to explicit symbolic relationships could bridge the gap between human mathematical cognition and model outputs.",
  "method": "We introduce Graph-of-Thought (GoT) prompting, where LLMs are prompted to construct and maintain a dynamic variable dependency graph alongside their solution. At each reasoning step, the LLM generates (1) the mathematical operation, (2) an update to the explicit dependency graph (nodes: variables/results, edges: dependencies), and (3) an updated 'current state' table summarizing all known quantities. The prompt encourages referencing and updating previous results through the graph, ensuring consistency and enabling backtracking or correction. This approach is implemented via a structured prompt template and a lightweight graph serialization format.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate GoT prompting on GSM8k, MATH, and MATHCOMP datasets, comparing with CoT and Sketch-of-Thought as baselines. Metrics include final answer accuracy, consistency across intermediate steps, and error localization capability. Ablation studies assess the impact of graph prompting on various problem classes and LLM sizes."
}
